title: A Journey Through Infertility
description: An interactive journey about IVF, told from two perspectives.
byline2: With Jan Diehm and Michelle Pera-McGhee
byline3: Illustrations by Rose Wong

landing.text: This is an interactive story about In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) told from two perspectives:

text.value: I never thought becoming a mom was going to be an issue for me. I came from a fertile group of women: My paternal grandmother had 13 children and my mother‚Äôs mom had 6.
But alas, I was not able to become pregnant after months of trying, and my doctor sent me on the route of IVF.

mom.description: An illustrated person walks onscreen, onto a winding path.

text.value: IVF is an obstacle course of logistics that you have to overcome while living your day-to-day life.
For the past two years, IVF has tested my physical and emotional stamina. It has drained my savings. And I‚Äôve been heartbroken again and again.

mom.description: She walks through a heart, which breaks in two as she passes.

text.value: Many women who‚Äôve done IVF call themselves warriors.
And I can see why: This whole journey has felt like a quest during which I‚Äôve had to put on a brave face and don armor as I took on one challenge after another.

mom.description: She dons armor, like a warrior.

text.value: First, the five egg retrievals.

To try and produce as many eggs as my body could muster, I became a science experiment: I injected myself with a cocktail of hormones meant to mirror the body‚Äôs natural reproductive signals, gave my doctor a blood sample every other day, and had an ultrasound technician monitor my ovaries.

mom.definition-retrievals: An egg retrieval is a procedure that takes between 15 and 30 minutes during which a doctor removes mature eggs from a person‚Äôs ovaries.
mom.definition-cocktail: The synthetic hormones are meant to encourage the production of multiple eggs, prevent the premature release of eggs or ovulation and optimize the timing of the egg retrieval.
mom.description: She covers her head with her hands as needles descend on her.

text.value: Over the course of five retrievals, I was stabbed with 932 needles.

deep-needles.value: My veins were poked 79 times for blood samples.

value.alt: a grid of 79 illustrated yellow needles

deep-needles.value: I stabbed myself with 166 syringes to self-inject hormones.

value.alt: a grid of 166 illustrated coral needles

deep-needles.value: And because I wanted to improve my odds, I went to 31 acupuncture appointments, where 687 needles pierced my underbelly, legs and head. My doctors had varying opinions about how much acupuncture, but my acupuncturists told me it helped with hormone regulation and blood flow.

value.alt: a grid of 687 illustrated purple needles

mom.description: She covers her head with her hands as more needles descend on her.

mom.text: Each cycle was also a test of my ability to handle logistics. There were the 7am doctor‚Äôs appointments before work; the dozens of days working from home to receive all my medications; and the many times I reshuffled my travel plans. I‚Äôve become a master of schedule Tetris.

deep-logistics.value: Over 20 months, I navigated 100+ appointments, averaging one per week. During my six busiest months (including late 2024 and much of 2025), I averaged 10+ visits a month‚Äîmeaning I spent one out of every three days in a doctor‚Äôs office.

value.alt: a calendar heatmap of doctor‚Äôs appointments

mom.description: She walks past a big calendar showing a chaotic mess of appointments.

text.value: There are adversaries, too: the insurance agent who wrongfully denied my claim; the person from the finance department who overcharged me multiple times; and the administrator who wanted $7 to release my digital health records.

deep-claim.value: According to the Kaiser Family Foundation, 23 states (including DC) require private insurance companies to cover fertility-related treatment, though the details may vary.

value.alt: a U.S. map where states are colored by if private insurance covers (yellow, 23 states) or does not cover (purple, 28 states) fertility-related treatment

deep-claim.value: And, 18 states (including DC) require Medicaid to cover fertility-related treatment, though Medicaid managed plan benefits may vary.

value.alt: a U.S. map where states are colored by if medicaid covers (yellow, 18 states) or does not cover (purple, 33 states) fertility-related treatment

mom.description: She is surrounded by icons representing these adversaries.

text.value: There‚Äôs also so much that can go wrong.
One time, my fertility clinic did not send in my prescription on time. I had to call the pharmacy 32 times to get my medication approved.

mom.text: Another time, I was sent to the wrong clinic for an appointment.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and when she reaches the edge of the path, the floor ahead falls out from underneath her.

text.value: And yet another time, I received a text message that falsely claimed I had $10,000 in medical debt. All this, while I was still sobbing over a failed IVF cycle.

mom.description: She continues to walk, and bills pile up alongside the path.

mom.text: I‚Äôve persevered for so long, spent so much money, arrived at every appointment on time.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and buries her face in her hands, crying.

text.value: And even after I did everything right, I was still foiled by bad luck, a dark cloud.
The majority of my embryos were abnormal. And 3 of the embryos I was able to transfer into my uterus failed to implant.

mom.description: She cries and walks, and a cloud appears over her head.

text.value: I have had small wins that kept me going and I‚Äôve lost big when I felt I was close to the goal.
Sometimes it feels like I‚Äôm stuck, while the world keeps moving on. Six friends have gotten pregnant since I started this journey ‚Äî two of them through IVF.

mom.text: But I‚Äôve also met so many people on my quest who‚Äôve pushed ahead for me when I couldn‚Äôt muster the strength.
mom.description: She continues to walk, looking hopeful.

mom.text: There is Simone, a friend and fellow IVF warrior, who shared so many little crucial tips and helped me keep going.
mom.description: She continues to walk, and a friendly figure appears hovering above her.

mom.text: There are my other fellow warriors: a friend and her wife who conceived their daughter through IVF; a friend‚Äôs wife who sent me a gift during a tender time; a friend who‚Äôs freezing her eggs to preserve her fertility. We walked alongside one another.
There are the many women on Reddit, on Facebook and in Google Groups, who encourage each other, share knowledge, and console one another.

deep-encourage.value: IVF-related reddit groups Facebook groups abound online. They can be as specific to your condition, like "IVF SUPPORT own eggs 40+" (for women who are 40 and older and want to use their own eggs) or "IVF with Endometriosis" or they can be location-specific. While it can be a lot to read about and hold the collective anxieties of dozens of strangers, I found that the posts asking for stories of successful pregnancies to be the sweetest.

"Just need some encouragement here and was wondering if anyone here has any success stories? Thank you Ladies ‚ù§Ô∏è" one user asked after sharing her story. The responses are full of specifics of each person's journey but also full of expressions of care.

value.value: Sorry to hear you‚Äôre in pain.
I can understand deeply the longing to find success stories of women in your shoes and how hard it is to hold onto hope. Hang in there!
Everyone‚Äôs story is going to be different but there is hope for everyone who has a uterus and ovaries.
fertility treatment is hard. Take care ‚ô•Ô∏è

text.value: I've built my own little band of warriors. We cheer each other on. It's a shitty club none of us want to be part of, but one that is full of the best people.

mom.text: This can feel like a lonely journey. But when I look around, I know my village and I are ready to meet my child when the time comes.

beats.description: She walks through a door, comes out the other side, and meets the child, who has journeyed from the other side, in the middle.

text.value: My story begins as a cell.
With a lot of help and a little luck, I‚Äôll develop into an egg, then an embryo, and eventually a baby.
My mom struggles with infertility, so she started In vitro fertilization or IVF to give me a boost.

baby.description: An illustrated follicle rolls onscreen, onto a winding path.

baby.text: Right now, I‚Äôm tucked inside one of her follicles. It‚Äôs a busy neighborhood here in the ovaries and dozens of us are waiting for our chance. Each month, only one follicle is chosen to nourish an egg until it‚Äôs ready to be released. Every time a neighbor leaves, I wave goodbye and wish them luck, then watch as a new follicle settles in.
baby.definition-follicles: Ovarian follicles are sacs filled with fluid that have the potential to release an egg. Follicles are often used to assess fertility and fertility treatment.
baby.description: Several more follicles appear surrounding it.

baby.text: The odds of one lonely egg becoming a healthy baby are slim, especially since Mom is in her late 30s. As hard as she and my dad tried, the eggs she released each month did not end up in a pregnancy.
baby.definition-egg: The human egg, or ovum, together with sperm, is vital to human reproduction. Women are born with about 1-2 million eggs which they release over their lifetime. Each month, women of reproductive age usually release one egg.
baby.description: The follicles travel along the path.

deep-births.value: A 2017 study found that the ‚Äúbiological clock‚Äù ticks much faster for women. It‚Äôs measured using a fecundability ratio, or the monthly likelihood of conception. For men, the odds remain nearly the same from age 20 to 45. But, women see a sharp decline ‚Äî a 45-year-old woman has less than half the chance of conceiving per month compared to a 21-year-old.

value.alt: a line chart showing the monthly likelihood of conception, or fecundability, by age and gender. as men age, they have roughly the same odds of conception as they did when they were 21, while women‚Äôs chances drop off as they age ‚Äî a 45-year-old woman has less than half the chance of conception compared to a 21-year-old woman.

deep-births.value: SOURCE: Age and fecundability in a North American preconception cohort study, 2017

baby.text: That‚Äôs where IVF can help. Instead of following the "one at a time" rule, a whole group of us were able to mature at once. The more eggs, the better the odds, and the more goodbye waves I had to give.

deep-ivf.value: IVF involves three steps: an egg retrieval, egg fertilization and an embryo transfer.
During egg retrievals, women are instructed to inject hormones every day into their lower abdomen to encourage the growth of as many follicles as possible to produce as many eggs as possible.
Once the eggs are mature (roughly 2 weeks after your period started), they are retrieved by a doctor.
An embryologist fertilizes each egg, and then observes them for 5-6 days.
Not all eggs fertilize to become embryos or make it to the 5th day. Those that do make it are graded (yes! Letter grades like As,Bs and Cs) and then generally frozen.
One of the embryos is then transferred back into the uterus of the willing parent (this also involves lots of hormone shots and pills).

baby.description: The follicles travel along the path.

baby.text: During her first round of IVF, my mom started with 8 follicles. After 14 days of hormonal injections, 7 produced eggs.
All 7 of those eggs were mature enough to be retrieved by her doctor.
And, 6 of those eggs were successfully fertilized by an embryologist. For 5 days, they were a small, hopeful group.
baby.description: 6 of the eggs are successfully fertilized.

baby.text: An embryologist watched the embryos grow into a more mature stage called a blastocyst, but not all of them made it through the grading.
baby.definition-blastocyst: A blastocyst is a cluster of cells that forms after an egg is fertilized. It‚Äôs the early stage of an embryo.

deep-grading.value: Embryos are graded using 3 criteria and given 3-digit alpha-numeric codes.

value.value: Blastocyst expansion (first number): graded from 1 to 6, with 6 being the most expanded from the embryo
Inner cell mass (middle letter): graded from A to C, with A being the highest quality with the most tightly packed cells
Trophectoderm (last letter): graded from A to C, with A being the highest quality with many cells forming a cohesive layer

value.alt: a heatmap showing day 5 embryo grading. excellent embryos are concentrated in the mid left with classifications like 3AA, 3AB, 4AA, 4AB, 5AA, 5AB

deep-grading.value: Embryos with higher grades have a stronger chance of implantation, while those with lower grades are more fragile.

value.alt: 2 bars charts showing the pregnancy rates by embryo grade (excellent: 65%, good: 59%, average: 50%, poor: 33%) and live birth rates by embryo grade (excellent: 50%, good: 50%, average: 42%, poor: 25%)

baby.description: The eggs turn into blastocysts, and only 3 make it.

text.value: Then we face another crucial hurdle: genetic testing. Since my mom is older than 35, doctors recommend these tests.
After an agonizingly long 21-day wait, we learned that all 3 of my mom‚Äôs embryos were abnormal and would likely lead to miscarriages if implanted.

baby.definition-genetictest: Genetic testing or PGTA (Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Aneuploidy) testing checks an embryo for abnormalities in the number of chromosomes. Health professionals and hopeful parents have varying opinions about the efficacy and ethics of this testing.
baby.description: The 3 blastocysts are no longer viable, and turn grey.

text.value: To me, IVF seems like a cruel lottery system drawn out over weeks.
Each cycle takes about 40 days from start to finish. If it doesn‚Äôt result in an implantable embryo, you start over. Back to a follicle.

baby.description: Back to one follicle, which continues down the path.

text.value: My mom braved this process 5 times.

deep-times.value: In total, my mom produced 57 eggs. Of the resulting embryos, 21 developed into blastocysts, and 4 were confirmed to have the correct number of chromosomes and were suitable to transfer as euploids. But, heartbreakingly, none of them implanted.

value.alt: a funnel visual for each of the 5 IVF cycles. cycle 1: 7 eggs, 3 blastocytes, 0 euploids; cycle 2: 9 eggs, 4 blastocytes, 0 euploids; cycle 3: 14 eggs, 7 blastocytes, 2 euploids; cycle 4: 18 eggs, 4 blastocytes, 2 euploids; cycle 5: 9 eggs, 3 blastocytes, 0 euploids

text.value: When it was finally time to transfer an embryo, Mom underwent a battery of tests to make sure the new neighborhood in her uterus was ready for them.

Everything looked clear, but the first embryo didn‚Äôt implant. Neither did the next. That‚Äôs when Mom learned about her endometriosis‚Äîa condition that made her uterus a lot less hospitable than we‚Äôd hoped. The next ‚Äúmove-in day‚Äù transfer was delayed.

baby.definition-endometriosis: Endometriosis is a condition that causes tissue similar to the uterine-lining to grow in other parts of the body, leading to inflammation and scar tissue. It can cause pain and heavy periods, and is a common cause of infertility.

text.value: It‚Äôs hurdles like these ‚Äî the abnormal embryos, the failed implantations, the silent losses ‚Äî that make this journey feel like an uphill climb for people like my mom. She‚Äôs doing everything she can to help one little egg make it to birth.
Thankfully, she‚Äôs not alone.
Yes, there are the doctors, nurses, sonographers and other clinical staffers who are helping as much as they can. But there are others, too.

baby.description: One embryo continues down the path.

baby.text: There was the nutritionist who coached my mom on what to eat to help make a healthy embryo.
baby.description: The embryo continues on, and a friendly figure appears above it.

baby.text: There was the fertility doula‚Äîpart strategist, part shield. She helped Mom interpret each test result and helped her find a better clinic. She even went to battle with the finance departments alongside my mom, saving thousands of dollars.
There were the acupuncturists who channeled a lot of medicinal wisdom from East Asia and adjusted their treatment to each phase of my mom‚Äôs journey.
And there was the microbiologist who wrote a whole book in which she laid out what supplements and foods may be helpful.

Even with the help of all these people, along with specialized treatments for her endometriosis and 2 more transfers, she‚Äôs still fighting to get pregnant. I hope to be the one who will finally help my mom conceiveg.
baby.description: The embryo continues along with the 4 figures hovering above them.

baby.text: I know this feels like a lonely journey for my mom. But when I look around, I know that there‚Äôs a whole village waiting to meet me when the time comes.
baby.description: The child walks through a door, comes out the other side, and meets the mom, who has journeyed from the other side, in the middle.

methods.value: You can make headers and paragraphs here.
